items.subtopic: Testicular Cancer | INCIDENCE AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology and Incidence of Testicular Germ Cell Tumors (GCTs)
items.content: Testicular GCTs are predominantly seen in adolescent and young adult males, with a notable increase in incidence worldwide. The highest rates occur in Scandinavia, Western Europe, and Australia/New Zealand, while Africa and Asia have the lowest rates. In 2023, approximately 9200 cases will be diagnosed in the United States, with about 470 deaths.
keyPoints: Testicular GCTs are the most common malignancy in males aged 15–39 years.
~9200 new cases of testicular GCTs expected in the US in 2023.
470 deaths anticipated from testicular GCTs in the US in 2023.
Highest incidence in Scandinavia, Western Europe, and Australia/New Zealand; lowest in Africa and Asia.
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · Testicular Cancer | INCIDENCE AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Testicular Cancer | INCIDENCE AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Risk Factors for Testicular Germ Cell Tumors (GCTs)
items.content: Key risk factors for testicular GCT include a prior history of the disease, cryptorchidism, and testicular germ cell neoplasia in situ. Men with a previous testicular GCT have a 1–2% risk of developing a contralateral tumor. Cryptorchidism increases the risk by four to six times, although orchidopexy before puberty reduces but does not eliminate this risk.
keyPoints: Prior history of testicular GCT carries a 1–2% risk for contralateral GCT.
Cryptorchidism results in a four- to six-fold increased risk of developing testicular GCT.
Orchidopexy before puberty reduces but does not eliminate the risk associated with cryptorchidism.
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · Testicular Cancer | INCIDENCE AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Genetic Markers in Germ Cell Tumors (GCTs)
items.content: Understanding the genetic markers associated with GCTs is crucial for assessing heritable risk and guiding diagnosis. Most malignant GCTs originate from germ cell neoplasia in situ, with an excess copy number of isochromosome 12p being a common molecular event. The KITLG locus on chromosome 12 has been identified as the strongest genetic association with testicular GCT risk.
keyPoints: Germ cell tumors (GCTs) originate from primordial germ cells.
Most malignant GCTs arise from in situ neoplasia.
Excess copy number of isochromosome 12p (i[12p]) is common in GCTs.
KITLG locus on chromosome 12 is the strongest genetic risk factor for testicular GCT.

items.title: Classification and Treatment of Germ Cell Tumors (GCTs)
items.content: GCTs are classified into seminomas and nonseminomas, with distinct clinical features and treatment approaches. Seminomas, accounting for ~50% of cases, occur primarily in the fourth decade and are sensitive to chemotherapy and radiation. Nonseminomatous GCTs, diagnosed mostly in the third decade, include subtypes like embryonal carcinoma, yolk sac tumor, choriocarcinoma, and teratoma, with varying sensitivities to treatment.
keyPoints: GCTs are classified as seminomas or nonseminomas.
~50% of GCT cases are seminomas, occurring in the fourth decade.
Seminomas do not secrete AFP but may secrete $\beta$-hCG.
Nonseminomatous GCTs are diagnosed primarily in the third decade.
Embryonal carcinoma may secrete AFP and/or hCG.
Yolk sac tumors often secrete AFP.
Choriocarcinoma secretes high levels of hCG.
Teratomas, resistant to chemotherapy, require surgical treatment.

items.title: Recognizing Testicular Cancer Presentation: Pathognomonic Features and Differential Diagnosis
items.content: A painless testicular mass is pathognomonic of a germ cell tumor (GCT), but most patients present with swelling, firmness, or discomfort. Differentials include epididymitis or orchitis; if unresponsive to antibiotics, scrotal ultrasound is warranted.
keyPoints: Painless testicular mass is pathognomonic of GCT
Most common presentations: swelling, firmness, discomfort
Differential diagnosis includes epididymitis or orchitis
Scrotal ultrasound recommended if no response to antibiotics

items.title: Diagnostic Approach and Serum Markers in Testicular Cancer
items.content: In suspected testicular GCT, perform scrotal ultrasound for firm masses. Measure serum AFP (elevated ~60–70% in non-seminomatous), hCG (elevated in both types), and LDH. Seminomas do not secrete AFP; elevated AFP suggests nonseminomatous GCT.
keyPoints: Scrotal ultrasound for firm testicular masses
AFP elevated ~60–70% in non-seminomatous GCTs, seminomas never secrete AFP
hCG can be elevated in both non-seminomatous and seminomas
LDH is nonspecific but helps assess metastatic risk

items.title: Why perform radical inguinal orchiectomy for suspected testicular GCT?
items.content: Radical inguinal orchiectomy is the initial treatment of choice for patients suspected of having a testicular germ cell tumor (GCT). This procedure involves removal of the affected testicle and spermatic cord up to the internal inguinal ring. It is preferred because it allows pathologic examination of the entire testicle, which is crucial given that GCTs can be multifocal.
keyPoints: Radical inguinal orchiectomy is the gold standard for initial treatment.
Removal includes the testicle and spermatic cord to the internal inguinal ring.
Pathologic examination of the entire testicle is essential due to potential multifocality.

items.title: How is staging performed for testicular GCT?
items.content: Staging of testicular germ cell tumors (GCTs) involves understanding their pattern of spread. Initially, these tumors spread lymphatically to retroperitoneal nodes, with left-sided tumors affecting the paraaortic nodes and right-sided tumors affecting aortocaval nodes. Imaging includes CT scans of the abdomen and pelvis for all newly diagnosed cases, and chest x-rays are also performed. Further imaging such as CT of the chest is indicated if retroperitoneal metastases or lung nodules are identified.
keyPoints: Staging involves understanding lymphatic spread patterns: left-sided to paraaortic nodes; right-sided to aortocaval nodes.
CT scans of abdomen and pelvis, plus chest x-ray, are standard for newly diagnosed cases.
Further imaging (e.g., CT chest) is indicated if retroperitoneal metastases or lung nodules are present.

items.title: Management of Stage I Seminoma
items.content: Stage I seminoma is primarily managed through active surveillance following orchiectomy. This approach involves regular physical exams and CT scans, with curative treatment available for the small percentage who develop metastatic disease during surveillance.
keyPoints: 70% of newly diagnosed patients present with stage I seminoma.
15% may have microscopic metastatic disease in the retroperitoneum.
Active surveillance is common post-orchiectomy, involving interval physical exams and CT scans.
Curative treatment is nearly always successful for those who develop metastasis during surveillance.
Adjuvant carboplatin monotherapy can decrease recurrence risk but is often unnecessary as orchiectomy alone cures most patients.

items.title: Management of Stage I Nonseminomatous GCTs
items.content: Stage I nonseminomatous GCT management depends on risk factors such as lymphovascular invasion. Active surveillance is common, but adjuvant chemotherapy or prophylactic RPLND may be considered for higher-risk patients.
keyPoints: 40% of newly diagnosed patients present with stage I nonseminomatous GCTs.
Normalization of serum tumor markers post-orchiectomy is required to confirm stage I.
Stage IS disease requires cisplatin-based chemotherapy if markers remain elevated.
Risk of recurrence is ~20% without lymphovascular invasion, and ~50% or higher with high-risk features.
Prophylactic RPLND can be diagnostic and therapeutic but involves major surgery risks.
Active surveillance is common, especially for low-risk patients.
Adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy (BEP) may be used for higher-risk stage I nonseminomatous GCTs.

items.title: Management of Stage II Seminoma
items.content: Stage II seminoma treatment varies by node size, with options including dogleg radiation therapy for stage IIA and cisplatin-based chemotherapy or radiation for stages IIB and IIC.
keyPoints: 15–20% of newly diagnosed patients present with stage II seminoma.
Stage IIA involves nodes ≤2 cm treated with dogleg radiation therapy.
Cisplatin-based chemotherapy is an option for stage IIA.
Stage IIB can be managed with cisplatin-based chemotherapy or, selectively, radiation therapy.
Most relapsed patients post-radiation are cured with subsequent cisplatin-based chemotherapy.
RPLND may be considered in select cases of stage IIA and nonbulky stage IIB to avoid chemo/radiotherapy.
Stage IIC requires cisplatin-based chemotherapy.

items.title: Cisplatin-based Chemotherapy for Stage III GCT: Risk Stratification and Treatment Protocols
items.content: Stage III germ cell tumors (GCTs), both seminoma and nonseminomatous, are treated with cisplatin-based chemotherapy. Patients are categorized into good-, intermediate-, or poor-risk groups using the International Germ Cell Consensus Classification system based on factors like histology, primary site, metastatic disease presence, and serum tumor markers post-orchiectomy. Good-risk patients have a >90% cure rate, while intermediate- and poor-risk patients have 5-year survival rates of ~80% and 50%, respectively.
keyPoints: Stage III GCT treated with cisplatin-based chemotherapy
Risk stratification: good-, intermediate-, poor-risk using International Germ Cell Consensus Classification
>90% cure rate for good-risk, ~80% survival for intermediate-risk, 50% for poor-risk over 5 years
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · STAGE-BASED MANAGEMENT (part 2) | RELAPSED DISEASE

items.title: Chemotherapy Protocols and Monitoring in Stage III GCT Management
items.content: Good-risk metastatic GCT patients receive three cycles of BEP or four cycles of EP, while intermediate- and poor-risk patients are treated with four cycles of either BEP or VIP. Maintaining the chemotherapy dose and schedule is crucial as modifications can lead to worse outcomes. Serum tumor markers should normalize during or after treatment. Cisplatin-based therapy has side effects like myelosuppression, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, and sterility.
keyPoints: Good-risk: 3 cycles BEP or 4 cycles EP; Intermediate-/poor-risk: 4 cycles BEP or VIP
Maintain dose/schedule to avoid inferior outcomes
Monitor serum tumor markers for normalization during/after treatment
Side effects: myelosuppression, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, sterility
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · STAGE-BASED MANAGEMENT (part 2) | RELAPSED DISEASE

items.title: Postchemotherapy Surgery and Management of Residual Masses in Nonseminomatous GCT
items.content: After cisplatin-based chemotherapy, many patients with normalized serum tumor markers have residual masses. About half are necrosis/fibrosis; 40% have teratoma, and 10% viable nonteratomatous GCT. Residual masses in nonseminomatous GCT require surgical resection as imaging cannot differentiate between these entities. This often involves postchemotherapy RPLND, with thoracotomy or neck dissection if needed. For metastatic seminomas, residual masses ≤3 cm can be observed; >3 cm may need FDG-PET to assess viability.
keyPoints: Post-chemo: 50% necrosis/fibrosis, 40% teratoma, 10% viable GCT in nonseminomatous
Residual masses require surgical resection; imaging cannot differentiate entities
Metastatic seminomas with ≤3 cm residual masses can be observed; >3 cm may need FDG-PET
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · STAGE-BASED MANAGEMENT (part 2) | RELAPSED DISEASE

items.question: Which geographic region has the highest incidence of testicular germ cell tumors (GCTs)?
options: Scandinavia, Western Europe, and Australia/New Zealand
items.explanation: The passage states that the highest incidence of testicular GCTs is in Scandinavia, Western Europe, and Australia/New Zealand.

items.subtopic: Testicular Cancer | INCIDENCE AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: What is the estimated heritability of testicular germ cell tumors (GCTs)?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that testicular GCTs have an estimated heritability of almost 50%.
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · Testicular Cancer | INCIDENCE AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Testicular Cancer | INCIDENCE AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which population is most commonly diagnosed with testicular germ cell tumors (GCTs)?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that testicular GCT is most commonly diagnosed in Caucasians.
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · Testicular Cancer | INCIDENCE AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which genetic abnormality is most commonly associated with germ cell tumors (GCTs)?
items.explanation: Genetic analysis of GCTs has demonstrated an excess copy number of isochromosome 12p (i[12p]) in most cases.

items.question: Which marker is NOT secreted by seminomas?
items.explanation: Seminomas do not secrete $\alpha$-fetoprotein (AFP).

items.question: Which of the following histologic subtypes of nonseminomatous GCTs is known to be chemotherapy resistant?
items.explanation: Teratomas are chemotherapy resistant and must be treated surgically.

items.question: Which serum tumor marker is elevated in approximately 60–70% of patients with non-seminomatous germ cell tumors (GCTs)?
items.explanation: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is elevated in ~60–70% of patients with non-seminomatous GCTs.

items.question: What should be the next step if a firm testicular mass is identified during physical examination?
items.explanation: A scrotal ultrasound should be performed if a firm testicular mass is identified.

items.question: Which condition may present with gynecomastia in patients with elevated serum hCG?
items.explanation: Gynecomastia may be present in patients with seminomas and elevated serum hCG.

items.question: What is the initial treatment for most patients suspected of having a testicular germ cell tumor (GCT)?
options: Radical inguinal orchiectomy with removal of the testicle and spermatic cord to the level of the internal inguinal ring.
Orchiectomy deferred until completion of chemotherapy in all cases.
Testis-sparing surgery as the gold standard treatment.
items.explanation: Radical inguinal orchiectomy is the initial treatment for most patients suspected of having a testicular GCT.

items.question: Where should metastatic disease from a right-sided testicular GCT first spread to?
options: Left paraaortic lymph nodes inferior to the left renal vessels.
Aortocaval nodes inferior to the right renal vessels.
Inguinal lymph nodes if scrotal violation occurred.
items.explanation: Right-sided testicular GCTs first spread to the aortocaval nodes inferior to the right renal vessels.

items.question: What imaging should be performed for newly diagnosed testicular germ cell tumors?
options: Computed tomography (CT) scan of the abdomen and pelvis, chest x-ray.
Bone scan and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain routinely.
Positron emission tomography (PET) as part of initial staging.
Only a chest x-ray is necessary.
items.explanation: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis, along with a chest x-ray, should be performed for newly diagnosed testicular GCTs.

items.question: What is the most common approach for patients with clinical stage I seminoma following orchiectomy?
options: Adjuvant radiation therapy to the paraaortic lymph nodes
items.explanation: Active surveillance is the most common approach elected by patients following orchiectomy for stage I seminoma.

items.question: What treatment is recommended for patients with clinical stage IS nonseminomatous GCTs?
items.explanation: Patients with persistently elevated or rising serum tumor markers after orchiectomy have stage IS disease and should be treated with cisplatin-based chemotherapy.

items.question: What is the risk of recurrence for nonseminomatous GCTs limited to testis without lymphovascular invasion?
items.explanation: If the tumor is limited to testis without lymphovascular invasion, the risk of recurrence is approximately 20%.

items.question: Which treatment approach has been historically used for stage IIA seminoma?
items.explanation: Patients with stage IIA seminoma are usually treated with "dogleg" radiation therapy.

items.question: Which chemotherapy regimen is recommended for patients with good-risk metastatic GCT?
options: Three cycles of BEP or four cycles of etoposide and cisplatin (EP)
items.explanation: Patients with good-risk metastatic GCT are treated with either three cycles of BEP or four cycles of etoposide and cisplatin (EP).
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · STAGE-BASED MANAGEMENT (part 2) | RELAPSED DISEASE

items.question: What percentage of patients with stage III GCT present with good-risk disease?
items.explanation: Most patients with stage III GCT present with good-risk disease and >90% will be cured.
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · STAGE-BASED MANAGEMENT (part 2) | RELAPSED DISEASE

items.question: What is the approximate 5-year survival rate for patients with poor-risk metastatic GCT?
items.explanation: Patients with poor-risk disease have a 5-year survival rate of ~50%.
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · STAGE-BASED MANAGEMENT (part 2) | RELAPSED DISEASE

items.question: Which factor is NOT included in the International Germ Cell Consensus Classification for risk stratification?
options: Presence of nonpulmonary visceral metastatic disease
items.explanation: The classification is based on histology, site of primary, and the presence of nonpulmonary visceral metastatic disease.
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · STAGE-BASED MANAGEMENT (part 2) | RELAPSED DISEASE

items.statement: A distinct biology related to geography has been identified for testicular germ cell tumors (GCTs).
items.explanation: The passage states that while the incidence varies geographically, a distinct biology related to geography is not apparent.
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · Testicular Cancer | INCIDENCE AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Testicular Cancer | INCIDENCE AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: Orchidopexy before puberty eliminates the risk of developing testicular germ cell tumors (GCTs) in patients with cryptorchidism.
items.explanation: The passage notes that orchidopexy decreases but does not eliminate the risk.
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · Testicular Cancer | INCIDENCE AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Seminomas are most commonly diagnosed in the third decade of life.
items.explanation: Seminomas arise most commonly in patients in the fourth decade of life.
Embryonal carcinoma may secrete AFP, and yolk sac tumor often secretes AFP.

items.statement: Transscrotal aspiration or biopsy of a testicular mass should never be performed.
items.explanation: Such procedures may result in tumor seeding of the scrotum or inguinal lymph nodes.

items.statement: Most patients with lung metastases will have abnormal auscultation findings.
items.explanation: Most patients with lung metastases will have normal auscultation of the lungs.

items.statement: Pathologic examination of the entire testicle is important since testicular GCTs may be multifocal.
items.explanation: Testicular GCTs can be multifocal, making pathologic examination of the whole testicle crucial.

items.statement: PET has a significant role in the initial staging of testicular germ cell tumors.
items.explanation: PET has little role in the initial staging of testicular GCTs.

items.statement: Most patients who undergo prophylactic RPLND for nonseminomatous GCTs will require chemotherapy.
items.explanation: Most patients who undergo prophylactic RPLND will never require chemotherapy.

items.statement: Patients with stage I seminoma have a 15% risk of developing metastatic disease during active surveillance.
items.explanation: Approximately 15% of patients with stage I seminoma develop metastatic disease during active surveillance.

items.statement: Radiographic imaging can accurately differentiate between necrosis, fibrosis, and viable nonteratomatous GCT in residual masses.
items.explanation: Radiographic imaging cannot accurately differentiate between these entities.
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · STAGE-BASED MANAGEMENT (part 2) | RELAPSED DISEASE

items.statement: Approximately 80% of patients with normal baseline spermatogenesis will recover spermatogenesis after cisplatin-based chemotherapy.
items.explanation: Approximately 80% of these patients will recover spermatogenesis over a period of several years.
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · STAGE-BASED MANAGEMENT (part 2) | RELAPSED DISEASE

items.assertion: Adjuvant radiation therapy is no longer the standard treatment for stage I seminoma.
items.reason: Concerns about late radiation-induced secondary malignancies have led to a decline in its use.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why adjuvant radiation therapy is no longer standard.

items.assertion: Prophylactic RPLND is recommended for all patients with stage I nonseminomatous GCTs.
items.reason: It helps in diagnosing and treating potential metastases without the need for chemotherapy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because prophylactic RPLND subjects many to unnecessary surgery; not all patients require it.

items.assertion: Patients with metastatic seminoma and residual masses >3 cm should be considered for postchemotherapy surgery.
items.reason: FDG-PET may distinguish necrosis from viable seminoma in these patients.
items.explanation: For patients with residual masses >3 cm, FDG-PET is used to identify those who should be considered for postchemotherapy surgery.
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · STAGE-BASED MANAGEMENT (part 2) | RELAPSED DISEASE

items.assertion: Patients with rapidly progressive metastatic disease and life-threatening symptoms should not initiate chemotherapy without a tissue diagnosis.
items.reason: Cisplatin-based chemotherapy is emergently initiated even without a tissue diagnosis in such cases.
items.explanation: Select patients with rapidly progressive metastatic disease should emergently initiate cisplatin-based chemotherapy, even without a tissue diagnosis.
items.reference: Testicular Cancer · STAGE-BASED MANAGEMENT (part 2) | RELAPSED DISEASE
